<!-- Generated by scripts/build-skills.py from skills/github/dobby-update-pbi/SKILL.md — do not edit this copy; edit the source and regenerate. -->

Update or refine an existing GitHub Issue.

GitHub issues have a **single body field** (markdown), so there is no truncation or format-flag dance — `gh issue edit --body-file` handles everything. No helper scripts are required.

This skill has two modes:

| User intent | Mode | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| "Update issue 42 title to …" | **Field update** | Directly changes the specified fields (title, labels, body sections) |
| "Set acceptance criteria to …" | **Field update** | Edits the Acceptance Criteria section of the body |
| "Refine issue 42" | **Refinement** | Reads everything (body, comments, codebase), synthesizes a well-structured issue |
| "Improve this issue" | **Refinement** | Same as above |
| "Make issue 42 clearer" | **Refinement** | Same as above |

If the intent is ambiguous, ask the user: "Do you want to update specific fields, or do a full refinement?"

## ⛔ Critical Rules (read before every run)

1. **Issue bodies are a single markdown field.** Preserve sections the user didn't ask to change — fetch the current body, edit the targeted section, write the whole body back. Never blank out content the user didn't mention.
2. **Markdown, not HTML.** GitHub renders markdown natively — use `**bold**`, `- list items`, `### headings`. No inline HTML.
3. **Run commands standalone — no piping.** Every `gh` command uses `--json`/`--jq` for reliable parsing. Read the full JSON output and extract fields in your own reasoning; do not append `| jq`, `| Select-Object`, `| grep`, etc.
4. **Always edit via a temp file.** Use `gh issue edit <N> --body-file <path>` to avoid shell-quoting issues with large markdown bodies. Clean up the temp file afterward.
5. **Follow the template** when generating content — `templates/issue-template.md` (Description + Acceptance Criteria sections).

## Common Steps (both modes)

### 1. Validate Prerequisites

**1a. Check gh CLI**
```bash
gh --version
```
- If `gh` is not found → stop: "GitHub CLI is not installed."

**1b. Check authentication**
```bash
gh auth status
```
- If not authenticated → stop: "Run: `gh auth login`"
- Display the active GitHub user so the user can catch a wrong-account issue early.

### 2. Resolve Owner and Repo

Read the `github` block from `.dobby/config.json` for `owner` and `repo`. If missing, prompt and persist (same pattern as `dobby-create-pbi`).

### 3. Fetch the Current Issue

```bash
gh issue view <N> --repo "<owner>/<repo>" --json number,title,state,body,labels,milestone,url
```

Extract the title, body, labels, and state — so you can preserve content the user didn't ask to change. Display the current title and state to the user for confirmation.

**If the issue is Closed**, ask whether to proceed before editing.

**Then choose the mode** based on the user's intent (see mode table above).

## Field Update Mode

Use this mode when the user wants to change specific, known fields.

### 4. Determine What to Update

| Target | Command |
|---|---|
| Title | `gh issue edit <N> --title "<new-title>"` |
| Labels | `gh issue edit <N> --add-label "<label>"` / `--remove-label "<label>"` |
| Milestone | `gh issue edit <N> --milestone "<name>"` |
| Body (Description / Acceptance Criteria) | edit the markdown, write to a temp file, `--body-file` |

### 5. Update the Body

When updating a body section (e.g., "add acceptance criteria"), fetch the current body (step 3), merge the change into the right section, and write the full updated body to a temp file:

```bash
gh issue edit <N> --repo "<owner>/<repo>" --body-file <path-to-updated-body.md>
```

Combine simple-field flags into the same call where possible:
```bash
gh issue edit <N> --repo "<owner>/<repo>" --title "<new-title>" --add-label "<label>" --body-file <path>
```

**Clean up the temp file after a successful update.**

### 6. Verify

```bash
gh issue view <N> --repo "<owner>/<repo>" --json title,body,labels,url
```

Confirm the updated fields and report the issue URL.

## Refinement Mode

Use this mode when the user says "refine", "improve", or wants a well-structured issue built from all available context. This mode reads everything — body, comments, linked items, and the codebase — then synthesizes a complete, template-compliant issue.

### R1. Fetch Comments

```bash
gh issue view <N> --repo "<owner>/<repo>" --json comments --jq '.comments[] | {author: .author.login, date: .createdAt, body: .body}'
```

**⚠️ How to treat comments:** comments are **historical context and ideas** — NOT authoritative requirements. They may contain brainstorming, outdated observations, stakeholder feedback, technical notes, or rejected approaches.

**Rules for using comments:**
- Prefer the current issue body and explicit user instructions over comment content.
- If comments conflict with each other or with the body, surface the conflict and ask — don't silently choose.
- Extract useful information (requirement hints, edge cases) but don't treat every comment as a requirement.
- Recent comments generally carry more weight than older ones — use judgment.

### R2. Fetch Related Context (bounded)

- **Task-list references** in the body (`- [ ] #<M>`): for each (cap at 5), fetch title and state via `gh issue view <M> --json title,state`.
- **Linked PRs**: `gh api "repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/<N>/timeline" --jq '[.[] | select(.event=="cross-referenced") | .source.issue | select(.pull_request) | {number, title, state}]'`.

These are context only — they don't define the current issue's requirements.

### R3. Explore the Codebase

Search the repository for context relevant to the issue, guided by the title, body, and comment insights:
- Existing code, modules, or services mentioned in the issue or comments.
- Relevant docs, design notes, or test specs.
- Hidden complexity (e.g., "remove field X" but X is used in 12 places).

**Guardrails:** search on specific terms, not broad exploration; prefer existing docs/tests/code; do not infer product requirements solely from current implementation (code is how it IS, not how it SHOULD be); if code contradicts the issue, surface the discrepancy. Keep it bounded — a few minutes, then move on.

### R4. Read the Template

Read `templates/issue-template.md` to guide output structure (Description + Acceptance Criteria). The refined issue must follow this structure.

### R5. Synthesize the Refined Issue

Combine all gathered context, in priority order:
1. Explicit user instructions (highest)
2. Current issue body
3. Linked/parent context
4. Codebase findings (technical reality)
5. Comments (ideas — weigh, don't blindly include)

**The refinement should:** fill empty/incomplete sections, sharpen vague language into specific/testable requirements, add in-scope/out-of-scope boundaries, add discovered dependencies, convert scattered comment ideas into structured acceptance criteria (Given/When/Then), and preserve already-good content.

**The refinement should NOT:** invent requirements not supported by any input, silently resolve conflicts (ask instead), or remove content unless clearly wrong/outdated (and flag the removal).

### R6. Present the Proposed Refinement

Show the user the full proposed body before applying:

```markdown
## Proposed Refinement — Issue #<N>: "<title>"

### Sources used
- Body: <existing / empty>
- Comments: <N> comments (<M> found relevant)
- Linked: <items or "none">
- Codebase: <summary of findings>

### Assumptions made
- <assumptions not explicitly stated>

### Unresolved questions
- <conflicts or ambiguities needing user input>

---

### Proposed Issue Body
<full proposed body following the template: Description + Acceptance Criteria in Given/When/Then>
```

**If there are unresolved questions**, ask them now and incorporate the answers before proceeding. Then ask: **"Do you want me to apply this refinement to the issue?"**

### R7. Apply the Refinement

On approval, write the new body to a temp file and apply:

```bash
gh issue edit <N> --repo "<owner>/<repo>" --body-file <path-to-body.md>
```

Verify (R-step equivalent of step 6) and report the issue URL. Clean up the temp file.

## Error Handling

- **Wrong identity**: `gh auth status` shows the wrong account → suggest `gh auth switch`.
- **Issue not found**: clear message with the ID used.
- **Permission errors**: surface the underlying `gh` error; suggest checking repo access.
- **Comments fetch fails**: warn but continue refinement with available context (body only). Comments are valuable but not essential.

## Optional Quality Gate

After a successful update or refinement, suggest:

> **Optional:** Run `grill-pbi` to stress-test the refined requirements and acceptance criteria before moving to proposal generation.

Do not invoke `grill-pbi` automatically — only suggest it.

## Efficiency Notes

- Skip prerequisite checks if already validated earlier in the same session (e.g., a create-then-update flow).
- Combine title/label/body changes into a single `gh issue edit` call when possible.
- Don't prompt for fields the user already provided.

## Usage Examples

**By number:**
> Update issue 42 — add an acceptance criterion for keyboard navigation

**Refine:**
> Refine issue 42

**Set a field:**
> Set issue 42 labels to enhancement, ux
